title: Crypto Salary Privacy: Why Earned Coins Should Not Live in One Wallet
keyQuestions: Why does crypto salary privacy matter after getting paid?
What is the one-wallet problem for crypto income?
Should salary, spending, savings, and swap funds use the same wallet?
How can freelancers separate crypto payments more cleanly?
When can a swap help after receiving crypto salary?
What should users check before swapping salary funds?
quickSteps: Receive crypto salary into a work wallet instead of mixing it immediately with spending or savings.
Assign each amount a role before moving it: spending, savings, business, private cash flow, or swap preparation.
Use separate wallets for roles that should not share the same public history.
Check fees, minimum amounts, asset, network, and destination before swapping salary funds.
Keep private records, but avoid exposing one wallet as your full financial profile.

issueRouting.start: A user receives salary or freelance income in crypto and needs to decide how to separate work income, spending, savings, and swap preparation.

branches.if: The same wallet receives salary and handles personal spending.
then: Create a work wallet role for incoming salary or client payments.
Move only planned amounts into spending or swap preparation wallets.
Avoid using the salary wallet for random purchases, public invoices, or app testing.

branches.if: The user wants to swap part of the salary.
then: Decide what role the swapped funds should have after conversion.
Check the asset, network, destination wallet, fees, and minimum amount.
Convert only the portion that needs a new role instead of moving the full salary by default.

branches.if: The user receives small or split crypto payments.
then: Group decisions by purpose instead of moving every small payment immediately.
Check whether the amount is practical for the selected route.
Keep private records that explain salary fragments without exposing the full wallet profile.

riskNotes: One wallet can become a single public financial profile when salary, savings, spending, and swaps all pass through it.
Freelancers may reveal more business context than intended if several clients and personal payments touch the same wallet.
A swap is useful only when the user knows what role the funds should have after conversion.
Small salary fragments can lose value or create unnecessary activity if moved without checking route minimums and fees.
Private bookkeeping is useful, but screenshots, wallet balances, address lists, and route details should not become public context.
